item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show edit
- Hide

To link to other thematically similar musical content, I propose the following edit:

Interviews with the violin soloist, ███ ███████, revealed that SCP-1687 was gifted to him by an anonymous benefactor, with a note instructing him to "play it well and keep the life of music flowing."

to

Interviews with the violin soloist, ███ ███████, revealed that SCP-1687 was gifted to him by an anonymous benefactor, with a note instructing him to "play it well and keep the life of music flowing."1

Footnotes
1. Investigation of this unknown Person of Interest has been assigned to MTF Eta-11 ("Savage Beasts").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're cool. It affects instruments only. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens to vocal accompaniment? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes yes yes.

If there is anything I love it is classical music and violins! Mostly upright bass but that is neither here nor there.

All hail the vampiric violin!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much. Think of it like this: have you ever changed stations on the radio and tuned into the new channel in the middle of a song you didn't recognize? I don't know about you, but for me there's like 1/4 of a second where I don't recognize it as music OR noise, neither melodious or discordant, but just as contextless, contentless, meaningless sound. Then after my brain takes that 1/4 second to realize that this is a song it isn't pattern-matching, the sound clicks back to being interpreted as music. Whatever the violin is doing to the other instruments puts them in that "non-music" state permanently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it alters some intangible "source" quality of the sound waves that can be percieved but not measured by any means whatsoever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Losing the music is a gradual process, so while the musicians heard that the orchestra as a whole was going downhill, they were panicking and trying to play better and were kinda in denial that their instruments were going dead too. "Mine only sounds crappy because it's getting overwhelmed by everyone else's crap! If I play harder it'll get better!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Recordings made before they were affected, no. Recordings made after they were affected, yes. It's a change in the instruments themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any attempts to play instruments so affected will produce sounds that are sonographically identical to music as determined by computer analysis, but that no living organism will recognize as music.

Does this apply to recordings of the instruments, or only live performances?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is amazingly creative. I can tell a lot of thought was put into this.

Egotistical Violins. Genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interviews with the violin soloist, ███ ███████, revealed that SCP-1687 was gifted to him by an anonymous benefactor, with a note instructing him to "help his musical brethren become Cool".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I correct in assuming that people playing instruments in the vicinity of the violin do not have their perception affected, at least until they stop playing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit, it's a musical vampire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just used my Mod-ly powers to remove the triple post. Don't worry about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I play in a chamber group and I would be sad if all my violin bros couldn't play with me anymore. ;;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But Zyn, you'd be safe! It's the people playing WITH you that need to worry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Wikidot's a bit buggy like that. Don't worry over it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it as a draft, like it now. The title of the review made me grin. Also, being a violinist myself, I'm unnerved by the concept. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't you let this thing get anywhere near my trombone! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is brilliant, an instrument that literally seeps music out of it surroundings to regenerate itself. Is this some kind of glitch on the forums?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted mainly for the mental image of a mass of bees playing the violin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The review made me want to punch the guy who wrote it, right in the face.

So for me, it's a good imitation of actual music reviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Those certainly are good questions. ;-)

Keep in mind that although detection equipment says that the waveforms are identical, it's specifically mentioned that plants react to it as if it were white noise. I.e. if you played the violin as part of a heavy metal band, the electric guitars, bass guitars and drums would all go "dead" as well, and there wouldn't be the expected "heavy metal" effect on plants.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A more technical description might be something along the lines of "Any musical instrument that is played with or in the immediate presence of SCP-1687 has its sound altered by a thus far unidentified mechanism. Any attempts to play…"
If the sound produced by affected instruments is acoustically identical to non-affected instruments, does the violin change the sound (or affect some non-acoustic property), or just the perception of that instrument's sound?
Also, is the quality-draining effect on other instruments permanent?
I'm not sure about the plant testing. Yes, plants are supposed to react differently to Bach than to heavy metal, but that's likely a result of the physical effect of the sound waves themselves, not an inherent quality of the type of music, so if this thing doesn't affect the sound waves themselves, it should have the same effect on a plant that a normal instrument would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the review quite a bit, myself. Made it easier to really feel what the description was getting at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone trolled a musician?
I like.
Also, the review is beautifully written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't find this very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: May have succeeded too well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was intentional. I don't know if you've read music critic reviews, but there are QUITE A LOT of pompous, pretentious reviewers who think it's their God-given duty to condescend to "the common people" in the name of "educating" them on what is and is not appropriate music/culture to enjoy. This reviewer is one of those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A selfish violin. Interesting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it hit the tone of an actual review reasonably well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The review was unbelievable cheesy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I put "musicality" in quotes because it's not really a scientific term, but what else would you call the effect?

As for the list of organisms, I was trying to go for a representative cross-section of things that have been proven to respond both positively and negatively to music, and different forms of music. And yes, I think I recall seeing some studies that show that houseplant growth rates actually do vary with statistical significance depending on whether they're grown in silent environments, environments with classical music (I think it was Bach?), and environments with rock/heavy metal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Couple things kinda stuck out to me, like the use of baseline and world-class, as well as "musicality", but other than that I loved it. There are far too little musical scips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"musicality"

I'm not sure the quotes are necessary here. I mean, is it musicality, except not musicality? Or is it being directly quoted from someone?

Tests using human beings, apes, canines, cetaceans, bees and plants

This seems like an oddly specific list. What, do cats not count? Why bees, but not grasshoppers?I understand human beings and apes, but why cetaceans? Why plants?

Other than that, there's very little that grabs me? I think it may just be an issue that I may not have enough sleep in me to appreciate this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this quite a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You would not BELIEVE the god-awful mainlist titles I tried to shoehorn on to this. Thank goodness I decided to not go for something cutesy.

Anyhoo, this SCP is dedicated to everyone who enjoys or performs classical music. Keep an ear out for that dead instrument!
